title: Local Slopes Multiply
concept: During backpropagation, each operation contributes its own local slope, and multiplying those tiny slope effects along the path gives the final gradient for an earlier weight.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In backpropagation, what does the chain rule follow from an early weight to the loss?

options.text: The connected chain of later operations.

exercises.explain: Correct! Backpropagation follows the path of connected operations from the weight to the final error.

cards.title: A hike made of many slopes
cards.body: Picture a long mountain trail. One section is steep, the next is gentle, and another is almost flat. Your total climb depends on all those local pieces together. Backpropagation works the same way: each step along the path contributes its own tiny slope.

cards.title: What is a local slope?
cards.body: A local slope tells us how sensitive one operation's output is to a tiny change in its input right there at that step. Backpropagation multiplies these local sensitivities along the route back to an earlier weight to get the final gradient.

cards.title: Valves in a water line
cards.body: Imagine water flowing through several valves. One valve doubles the flow, another cuts it in half, and a third barely lets anything through. The final water change depends on all the valves combined. Local slopes work like those valves for error signals.

cards.title: A three-step path
cards.body: Suppose one operation would double a signal, the next would halve it, and the next would triple it. The total effect is not any single step alone. Together they produce a 3-times effect because all the local contributions combine along the path.

cards.title: One weak link can shrink the whole signal
cards.body: People sometimes imagine the correction signal stays equally strong as it moves backward. Not always. If one local slope on the path is tiny, it can weaken the whole gradient a lot. This is one reason deep networks can be tricky to train.

cards.title: Strong path vs. weak path
cards.body: How local slopes shape the final gradient:

left.text:
Several steps pass along clear signal.
Final gradient stays noticeable.

right.text:
A nearly flat step weakens the chain.
Final gradient becomes very small.

exercises.prompt: Why does backpropagation care about local slopes at each step?

options.text: Because each step adds part of the final gradient for an earlier weight.
Because slopes are only used to draw charts for humans.
Because local slopes replace the loss function.
Because weights never affect one another through layers.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Each operation has its own sensitivity, and those sensitivities combine to form the gradient.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If one step in the backward path has an almost flat local slope, the final gradient for an earlier weight can become much smaller.
exercises.explain: True! A weak local slope can shrink the whole backward signal.

exercises.sentence: Backpropagation finds the final gradient by combining many local ___ along the path.
exercises.explain: Correct. Each local slope contributes to the total effect on the loss.

exercises.prompt: Which situation best shows local slopes multiplying?

options.text: Three linked valves each change the flow, so the final water change depends on all three together.
A model stores one photo in memory.

exercises.explain: Correct! Local slopes behave like linked valves that each shape the final signal.

exercises.prompt: An early hidden-layer weight affects the loss through three later operations. Two of those steps pass along strong correction signal, but one step has a very tiny local slope. What is the most likely result?

options.text: The final gradient for that early weight becomes much smaller because the tiny local slope weakens the whole chain.
The tiny local slope makes the final gradient automatically larger.
Local slopes do not affect gradients at all.
The loss node disappears and training stops.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Backpropagation multiplies local slope effects along the path, so one nearly flat step can shrink the final gradient a lot. Next, we will zoom back to the very start of the backward pass: the signal that leaves the loss node itself.
